request.question: What is the most important building in the National Heritage for England located 4 mi southwest of Congleton in Cheshire?

title: Listed buildings in Appleby-in-Westmorland
Listed buildings in Odd Rode
Congleton United Reformed Church
Church of St Thomas à Becket, Capel
Listed buildings in Congleton
Listed buildings in Edge, Cheshire
All Saints Chapel, Somerford
Nether Alderley Mill
St Peter's Church, Congleton
Little Moreton Hall
sentences: Appleby-in-Westmorland is a civil parish in the Eden District, Cumbria, England.
It contains 140 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England.
Of these, six are listed at Grade I, the highest of the three grades, nine are at Grade II*, the middle grade, and the others are at Grade II, the lowest grade.
The parish contains the market town of Appleby (renamed Appleby-in-Westmorland in 1974) and surrounding countryside.
There is a great variety of types of listed building in the parish.
The most important building is Appleby Castle; this and a number of associated structures are listed.
Most of the listed buildings are houses and associated structures, shops and public buildings.

Other listed buildings include churches, public houses, hotels, two crosses and a lamp post, farmhouses and farm buildings, former industrial buildings, schools, banks, a bridge, railway station buildings, and a milestone.
Odd Rode is a civil parish in Cheshire East, England.

It contains 35 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as designated listed buildings. Of these, one is listed at Grade I, the highest grade, five are listed at Grade II*, the middle grade, and the others are at Grade II.
The most important listed building in the parish is Little Moreton Hall; other notable country houses include Rode Hall and Ramsdell Hall.
Most of the other listed buildings are houses, farmhouses, and associated structures.
The Macclesfield and the Trent and Mersey Canals run through the parish, and the listed buildings associated with these are bridges, milestones, and distance markers.
The other listed buildings are a church.
a folly, wellheads, and a public house.
Congleton United Reformed Church is in Antrobus Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.
It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.
The Church of St Thomas à Becket is a redundant Anglican church in the village of Capel, Kent, England.
It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building, and is under the care of the Churches Conservation Trust.
Capel is located 4 mi east of Tonbridge, off the B2017 road.
It is said that Thomas Becket preached either in the church, or under a yew tree in the churchyard.
Congleton is a civil parish in Cheshire East, England.

It contains 133 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as designated listed buildings. Of these, one is listed at Grade I, the highest grade, four are listed at Grade II*, the middle grade, and the others are at Grade II.
The parish contains the town of Congleton, and surrounding countryside.
Passing through the parish are the Macclesfield Canal and the River Dane, and a number of listed structures are associated with these waterways.
The silk and cotton weaving industries came to the town from the 18th century, and there are listed buildings associated with these, including mills and weavers' cottages.
Otherwise the listed buildings include houses and cottages in the town and the country, churches and associated structures, shops, schools, a town hall, offices, and public houses.
Some of the buildings date from the 16th and 17th centuries, and are timber-framed.
The great majority of houses are from the late 18th and early 19th centuries, reflecting the industrial growth and prosperity in the town at this time.
Edge is a civil parish in Cheshire West and Chester, England.
It contains 18 buildings that are recorded in the National Heritage List for England as designated listed buildings.
One of these is listed at Grade II*, the middle grade, and the rest at the lowest grade, Grade II.
The parish is entirely rural, and most of the listed buildings are domestic, or related to farming.
The most important building is the country house, Edge Hall, which dates from about 1600.
Many of the other listed buildings are houses and cottages, either timber-framed or with a timber-framed core, dating from the 17th century.
Other structures in the list include a former corn mill, former stables, and a bridge near Edge Hall.
All Saints Chapel, Somerford is in an isolated position near the hamlet of Brereton Heath, between Congleton and Holmes Chapel, Cheshire, England.
It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.
The chapel is in the Anglican benefice of Astbury and Smallwood in the deanery of Congleton, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the diocese of Chester.
Nether Alderley Mill is a 16th-century watermill located in Congleton Road (the A34), to the south of the village of Nether Alderley, Cheshire, England.
It is owned by the National Trust, and is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.
It is a unique example of a triple overshot waterwheel system, two of which are in working order.
It is one of only four virtually complete corn mills in Cheshire.
St Peter's Church is in Chapel Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.
It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.
It is an active Anglican parish church in the Diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Congleton.
Its benefice is combined with those of St Stephen, Congleton, St John the Evangelist, Buglawton, and Holy Trinity, Mossley.
Alec Clifton-Taylor includes it in his list of 'best' English parish churches.
Little Moreton Hall, also known as Old Moreton Hall, is a moated half-timbered manor house 4 mi southwest of Congleton in Cheshire, England.

The earliest parts of the house were built for the prosperous Cheshire landowner William Moreton in about 1504–08, and the remainder was constructed in stages by successive generations of the family until about 1610.
The building is highly irregular, with three asymmetrical ranges forming a small, rectangular cobbled courtyard.
A National Trust guidebook describes Little Moreton Hall as being "lifted straight from a fairy story, a gingerbread house".
The house's top-heavy appearance, "like a stranded Noah's Ark", is due to the Long Gallery that runs the length of the south range's upper floor.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What is the most important building in the National Heritage for England located 4 mi southwest of Congleton in Cheshire?
hop1_docs: Congleton United Reformed Church | Congleton United Reformed Church is in Antrobus Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.

St Mary's Church, Congleton | St Mary's Church is in West Road, Congleton, Cheshire, England.  It is a Roman Catholic church recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.  The listing includes the adjoining presbytery.

Nether Alderley Mill | Nether Alderley Mill is a 16th-century watermill located in Congleton Road (the A34), to the south of the village of Nether Alderley, Cheshire, England.  It is owned by the National Trust, and is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.  It is a unique example of a triple overshot waterwheel system, two of which are in working order.  It is one of only four virtually complete corn mills in Cheshire.

St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton | The Church of St James and St Paul is situated to the south of the village of Marton, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Congleton.  Its benefice is combined with those of Holy Trinity, Capesthorne, Christ Church, Eaton, and All Saints, Siddington.  The church is an important location in the novel Strandloper by Alan Garner.

St Peter's Church, Congleton | St Peter's Church is in Chapel Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade I listed building.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the Diocese of Chester, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the deanery of Congleton.  Its benefice is combined with those of St Stephen, Congleton, St John the Evangelist, Buglawton, and Holy Trinity, Mossley.  Alec Clifton-Taylor includes it in his list of 'best' English parish churches.

All Saints Chapel, Somerford | All Saints Chapel, Somerford is in an isolated position near the hamlet of Brereton Heath, between Congleton and Holmes Chapel, Cheshire, England.  It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.  The chapel is in the Anglican benefice of Astbury and Smallwood in the deanery of Congleton, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the diocese of Chester.

St John the Evangelist's Church, Sandbach Heath | St John the Evangelist's Church is in Sandbach Heath, about 1 mi northeast of Sandbach, Cheshire, England.  It is an active Anglican parish church in the deanery of Congleton, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield, and the diocese of Chester.  Its benefice is united with those of Christ Church, Wheelock, and St Philip, Hassall Green.  The church is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.
hop1_titles: St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton
St John the Evangelist's Church, Sandbach Heath
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: listed buildings in and near Congleton, Cheshire.

- Congleton United Reformed Church — located "Antrobus Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England"; "designated Grade II listed building."
- St Mary's Church, Congleton — located "West Road, Congleton, Cheshire, England"; "Roman Catholic church"; "designated Grade II listed building"; "listing includes the adjoining presbytery."
- Nether Alderley Mill — "16th-century watermill" on "Congleton Road (the A34), to the south of the village of Nether Alderley, Cheshire, England"; "owned by the National Trust"; "designated Grade II* listed building"; "unique example of a triple overshot waterwheel system."
- St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton — "situated to the south of the village of Marton, Cheshire, England"; "designated Grade I listed building."
- St Peter's Church, Congleton — "Chapel Street, Congleton, Cheshire, England"; "designated Grade I listed building."
- All Saints Chapel, Somerford — "near the hamlet of Brereton Heath, between Congleton and Holmes Chapel, Cheshire, England"; "designated Grade II* listed building."
- St John the Evangelist's Church, Sandbach Heath — "about 1 mi northeast of Sandbach, Cheshire, England"; "designated Grade II listed building."

Ambiguities / missing facts:
- No passage states any site is "4 mi southwest of Congleton" — missing: the identity of the place or village that lies "4 mi southwest of Congleton" (bridge entity).
- No passage uses the phrase "most important building" in relation to the National Heritage List; the target building described that way is not identified here.
- Candidate nearby places from passages (ambiguous for 4 mi SW): Marton; Somerford / Brereton Heath; Nether Alderley — label these as candidate locations (ambiguous).

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1. Search: exact query "4 miles southwest of Congleton" or "4 mi southwest of Congleton Cheshire" (web/Wikipedia search) to identify the village/place at that location.
2. Fetch the Wikipedia page for the identified place (e.g., "Marton, Cheshire" or the village found) — inspect lede/infobox and "Listed buildings"/"Landmarks"/"History" sections for a sentence like "the most important building is..." and National Heritage List entries.
3. If place uncertain, fetch "List of listed buildings in [place]" pages (e.g., "Listed buildings in Marton, Cheshire", "Listed buildings in Somerford", "Listed buildings in Nether Alderley") and inspect lede/entries for Grade and phrasing "most important building."
4. Also fetch the pages for likely candidate buildings mentioned above (lede/infobox): "St James' and St Paul's Church, Marton"; "All Saints Chapel, Somerford"; "Nether Alderley Mill" to confirm proximity to Congleton and any "most important" phrasing.
hop2_docs: Smallwood, Cheshire | Smallwood is a civil parish and small village in the unitary authority of Cheshire East and the ceremonial county of Cheshire, England.  The village is situated approximately three miles east of Sandbach and 3 miles south west of Congleton.

Marton, Cheshire | Marton, Cheshire is a small village and civil parish in the unitary authority of Cheshire East and the ceremonial county of Cheshire, England on the A34 road 3 miles (5 km) north of Congleton (grid reference [ SJ850682] ).

South Cheshire College | South Cheshire College is a further education college, located in Crewe, England.  The College is a single campus situated in a residential area about one mile from Crewe town centre.  It also attracts students from Nantwich, Alsager, Middlewich, Sandbach, Congleton and throughout South and East Cheshire.  The College also provides courses for adults at a range of centres, including high street locations in Middlewich and Congleton.
Biddulph | Biddulph is a town in Staffordshire, England, 8.5 miles north of Stoke-on-Trent and 4.5 miles south-east of Congleton, Cheshire.

Arclid | Arclid is a village and civil parish in the unitary authority of Cheshire East and the ceremonial county of Cheshire, England.  It is about two miles east of Sandbach and five miles west of Congleton.  The parish had a population of 199 according to the 2001 census, increasing to 276 at the 2011 census.

Congleton Town F.C. | Congleton Town F.C. is a football club based in Congleton, Cheshire, England.  They currently play in the North West Counties League Premier Division and are full members of the Cheshire County Football Association.  The club have played in a number of regional leagues in the Cheshire area.

Siddington, Cheshire | Siddington is a civil parish in Cheshire, England.  It consists of farms; a picnic site; Redesmere, a half-mile long lake; and the Capesthorne Estate.  The village is at the crossroads of the A34 with the B5392 approximately halfway between Alderley Edge and Congleton.
response.summary_2: The provided passages do not identify a "most important building" 4 miles southwest of Congleton. Smallwood is noted as a village approximately three miles southwest of Congleton, but none of the passages names its principal listed building.
response.answer: I cannot determine the most important building from the provided summaries. Neither summary identifies a "most important building" 4 miles southwest of Congleton. Follow the retrieval steps above to locate the place 4 mi SW of Congleton and then check its Wikipedia/listed-buildings pages for the principal National Heritage-listed building.
